item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I’m a little confused by this one like why d-class wasn’t used (seems kinda like a waste of agents) and I Just wasn’t very engaged in this one

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Woohoo! An Okie!

My stepgrandparents used to live in Shawnee, and I really hated visiting there (mostly because I really didn't like that family). But Oklahoma always struck me as a place full of weird stuff that wasn't being explored enough, ergo, Pottawatomie.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
*Finds this on the SCP map as the only one located in Oklahoma*
Oh, cool! I live in Oklahoma!
*Pottawatomie County*
…I-I live in Pottawatomie County..
*..SCP-2132 will transport the nearest human being into the lounge..*
U-uh…starts to sweat

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Okay, I get most of this, but why were the agents the characters from Third Rock From The Sun?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I loved this but there's a small detail that bothers me or which may Yuki's voice appeared before the final described game, where there's some Arab writing. Is this a typo or was Assad "incorporated" into the game once he was relevated by Yuki?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Excellent. :D Pleasure working with you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
• Fix'd
  • Fix'd, that was how I initially had it
  • Cleared up
  • Cleared up
  • Yes (they have to have time to get the playground ready).
  • Fix'd

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You've definitely got the right attitude when it comes to taking criticism. :) I gave this another read, got through the whole thing, and found it effective, if slightly confusing. I did a bunch of guessing to make it through, but make it through I did, so +1. That said, I have a few more thoughts on things you can improve:

  • All instances of "county" should be capitalized.
  • I would like to see things presented in order: SCP-2132, then the -1. Starting with that doesn't really add anything that would be lost were it moved before the discovery paragraph. You would distinctly gain by opening with the more interesting bulk of the description, too.
  • I'm really confused as to what "lounge" is being referred to. I kept rereading the paragraph describing -1 because I thought maybe the answer was there, but it's not. (So if you meant to have it there, it would make sense to keep the -1 first.)
  • Also not sure if it's just "residents" who are affected, or anyone within the anomalous range.
  • There's an hour delay between retiring/being transported to the lounge and the game starting?
  • It would tighten things up just a smidge if you dropped the articles and conjuctions in the supplies listings. So "16 oz. can of Heinz baked beans, egg beater, 2 oz. bottle of ink, Craftsman brand screwdriver, fork."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very agreed, but T had a good point with tone and recovery info (plus, those weren't things I was super attached to, and I'm aware that tone is one of my weak points). I don't care if someone likes or dislikes something I write, but I do care that, if it's not super long, they finish it. It's my one piece of vanity, and something I'm trying to get over. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You're never going to make everyone like something. Some readers aren't happy unless an SCP is less than a page long, some readers aren't happy unless its over six pages long with eighteen collapsible addenda. If you're in the positive double digits after a few days, don't fix what isn't broken unless you're really sure of why it needs to be changed- that's a good way to break things.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I went through and fixed a few tonal things and moved the discovery sentence. I hope this helps you go back and make it to the end, the whole thing kind of hinges on the addendum.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That is as horrifying as it is plausible.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I felt like this had tonal issues ("don't"), plus putting the recovery info before the effect soured me on it. Don't have a lot else to say, save that I didn't make it to the end.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Another example of how I like to see MC&D - so rich, so decadent, that there is nothing left to do but play god for their entertainment and the entertainment of their patrons. Normally don't go for gameshow stuff but this was good.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I had no idea what was going on at first. I liked the Most Dangerous Gameshow aspect and was prepared to upvote based on that. Then I got to the powered by a forsaken child. Oof. Right in the horror gland (that's totally a real thing, by the way.) Enjoy your +1, you magnificent monster.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very novel concept, but I'll admit that I did have to read the spoilers before I fully understood what was going on. However, I liked it a lot, +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Which brings to mind the way MC&D sometimes 'acquires' their human assets…

Kids could have been collateral for their parents bills. Yech.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really like this one, even more after reading that disturbing ending, it really added to the piece. +1, awesome job!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, that was heartbreaking.

+1!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ding ding ding! Not reality benders, just forsaken kids.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Its a little confusing, I'll be the first to admit that.

Spoilers
– hide block

Inspiration-wise, I work with kids and I'm constantly astounded by the things they come up with. Like, kids are so weird and they can come up with such cool stuff. I'm very jealous. This also stems from my dad, who's one of those guys it's impossible to get presents for because he just goes and buys himself anything he wants. The stuff he wants us to get him are tickets to things, experiences. Human beings will never have everything they want, even after getting every experience, they'll always want something more specific, more catered, more unique. I also think numbers stations are creepy.

MC&D realized kids have a better imagination than they ever could, and decided to try to bottle that. The obstacle resort is fueled by a kid in said bottle, miles and miles under the hatch and radio tower, dreaming up ideas for new and exciting playgrounds that they can never play in. As'ad is one of a long line of kidnapped kids. However, as kids do, eventually As'ad grew up and got tired and couldn't come up with new ideas, so MC&D disposed of him and replaced him with Yuki. We'll see how long she lasts.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I get that the a kidnapped, lonely child is somehow a component of the SCP, but beyond that I can't figure it out.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Might not even be reality benders.
The horror lies in the part where the anomaly does all the bending and the kids are just used to think up things for it. Regular children 'procured' by MC&D in some manner or another.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you guys, my Japanese is secondhand. Fixed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Seconding this. Although the author might want to consider an honorific/suffix other than -san, depending on the intended perception of the message.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
About footnote 4:

"Welcome home, Yuki" in Japanese is written as お帰りゆきさん (Okaeri Yuki-san).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So Most Dangerous Game meets the holodeck powered by captive reality benders?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
And then came the bears.

Special thanks to DreamwalkerFae and psul for edits.

Picture is borrowed from Sam Stokes on Flickr.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's a heck of an ending. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was ready to give this a pass. Oh, dangerous game show-esque skip. It sounded somewhat similar to SCP-024 throughout (such is the nature of game show skips), with the most interesting slants being the inscriptions and the numbers station slant.

That last addendum though. It really raised the creepiness up in a way I didn't expect, and having it replaced with a new voice in a new language… damn. Now it makes me wonder how many times this has happened before when MC&D got a hold of it.

+1
